narrative_summary:
The BARD1 c.1670G>C (p.Cys557Ser) missense change has been investigated as a potential breast‑cancer and ovarian‑cancer susceptibility variant. It is a relatively common polymorphism in some populations (overall minor allele frequency ≈1.5 % in cancer‑free individuals, with frequencies ranging from 5 × 10⁻⁵ in East Asians to 2.4 % in Ashkenazi Jews and about 1 % in Finns) and has also been reported in prostate‑cancer cohorts as a variant of uncertain significance [PMC7464855][PMC10734870].

Clinical studies have produced conflicting risk estimates. Early case‑control analyses in Finnish, Nordic and Icelandic cohorts suggested an increased breast‑cancer or ovarian‑cancer risk for carriers, and an interaction with the BRCA2 founder mutation in Iceland that appeared to raise risk three‑fold [PMC7464855]. A Chilean family‑based study likewise reported an association with elevated breast‑cancer risk in families with strong history [PMC6410045]. Conversely, several larger meta‑analyses and case‑control series from Nordic, Australian, Canadian, Russian, English, Polish and other populations found no significant association, with a pooled odds ratio of 1.14 (95 % CI 0.94‑1.34, p = 0.13) indicating at most a low‑moderate risk [PMC7464855]. Additional Finnish case‑control data showed heterogeneous odds ratios, some reaching statistical significance and others not, underscoring the inconsistency of the epidemiologic signal [PMC7464855].

Functional investigations specific to this variant are limited. One review highlighted the paucity of functional data and noted that the variant has been listed among BARD1 missense changes without experimental validation, emphasizing the need for functional assays to clarify its effect [PMC6672023]. No experimental results directly supporting or refuting pathogenicity were presented in the extracted evidence.

Population‑level data confirm that the allele is present at variable frequencies across ethnic groups, with the highest prevalence in Ashkenazi Jews and Finns and the lowest in East Asians [PMC7464855]. In a targeted‑sequencing prostate‑cancer cohort the variant was identified and classified as a variant of uncertain significance, reflecting the lack of consensus on its clinical impact [PMC10734870]. Similar listings of the variant as a VUS appear in broader germline‑mutation surveys of Latin American breast‑cancer patients and colorectal‑cancer families, without further interpretation [PMC6827033][PMC5819082].

Overall, the evidence for BARD1 c.1670G>C (p.Cys557Ser) does not converge on a definitive classification. While some studies suggest a modest increase in breast‑cancer risk, especially in specific familial or population contexts, larger meta‑analyses and many case‑control investigations fail to demonstrate a statistically significant association. The variant’s relatively high population frequency in certain groups and its recurrent designation as a variant of uncertain significance further argue against a high‑penetrance pathogenic role. Consequently, the current data support a classification of BARD1 c.1670G>C as a low‑moderate risk allele or a variant of uncertain significance pending functional validation.

mentions.quoted_text: The first statistical association was reported by analyzing the BARD1 **c.1670G>C**; p.Cys557Ser variant in cancer cases from Finnish, Nordic, and Icelandic populations versus population-matched cancer-free controls. Collectively, these studies were in agreement with the possibility that carriers of this BARD1 variant had an increased risk to BC and/or OC. However, a negative association was reported by three different research groups that analyzed case-control groups from Finnish, Australian, and Polish populations. The inconsistency in results from the same Finnish population was explained by the possibility of a false positive association due to the small sample sizes. Indeed, using publicly available population based genetic databases the minor allele frequency (MAF) of BARD1 **c.1670G>C**; p.Cys557Ser is 0.015 in the general cancer-free population overall, but it is highly variable among different populations ( v2.1.1). It is the rarest (MAF = 5.2 x 10-5) in East Asians and the most common (MAF = 0.024) in the Ashkenazi Jewish population (Supplementary Table S1). In the Finnish population, this variant has a frequency of approximately 1%, which is relatively more common compared to known pathogenic variants in well-established BC and OC predisposing genes, BRCA1 or BRCA2 (0.001%). This suggests that the effect size of BARD1 **c.1670G>C**; p.Cys557Ser could be small, that is, it could confer a low-moderate risk to cancer relative to the high risk associated with BRCA1/BRCA2 pathogenic variants.

The contribution of BARD1 variants to cancer risk or disease progression in carriers of pathogenic variants in known cancer predisposing genes is unknown. Four studies of BC cases have investigated the co-occurrence of carriers of the BARD1 **c.1670G>C**; p.Cys557Ser variant with pathogenic variants in BRCA1 or BRCA2. The prevalence of this BARD1 variant was significantly higher in Icelandic BC cases who also carried the pathogenic BRCA2 c.771_775del; p.Asn257LysfsTer17 variant known to be a founder pathogenic variant in this population. The risk of BC associated with carrying both variants was estimated to be three-fold higher than carriers of BRCA2 c.771_775del; p.Asn257LysfsTer17 alone and the lifetime probability of developing BC approaches 100% in carriers of both variants. Three other studies of BC cases from Nordic, Polish and mixed populations found that carriers of the BARD1 **c.1670G>C**; p.Cys557Ser variant and a BRCA1 or BRCA2 pathogenic variant did not confirm this finding. Thus, further studies are needed to clarify the interaction of BARD1 with BRCA1 or BRCA2. It is possible that specific BARD1 variants have additive effects by increasing or modifying risk in a linear or synergistic manner in carriers of other high risk variants (reviewed in).

A number of studies estimated the cancer risk associated with specific variants in BARD1 in BC and hereditary BC and/or OC syndrome families (Table 2). We found reports describing the risk for nine different BARD1 variants: one nonsense, one frameshift, six missense and two synonymous variants. Risk estimates are variable across these studies, where the highest risk was observed for c.1690C>T; p.Gln564Ter in association with TNBC cases not selected for family history of cancer (OR 3.62, 95% CI 1.21-10.78, p = 0.02). This is consistent with the previous report suggesting that BARD1 variants may be associated with a higher risk for TNBC. A meta-analysis of the **c.1670G>C**; p.Cys557Ser variant in 11,870 BC cases and 7687 controls, from Nordic, Australian, Canadian, Russian, English, and Polish populations, concluded that carriers of this BARD1 variant have a low risk for BC (OR 1.14, 95%CI 0.94-1.34, p = 0.13). While studies estimating the familial risk of BARD1 variants to OC are still lacking, evidence is emerging that BARD1 is a potential moderate risk gene for TNBC.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The review summarizes mixed epidemiological and interaction data for BARD1 c.1670G>C, suggesting it may confer a low‑moderate breast‑cancer risk but overall evidence remains inconclusive.
article_mentions.title: Literature Review of BARD1 as a Cancer Predisposing Gene with a Focus on Breast and Ovarian Cancers

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identified the BARD1 c.1670G>C (p.Cys557Ser) missense variant in prostate cancer patients, reporting it as a VUS with reported allele frequencies in the cohort.
article_mentions.title: Germline variant screening with targeted next generation sequencing in prostate cancer: phenotype-genotype correlation

mentions.quoted_text: Despite these similarities, the clinical significance of BARD1 remains uncertain. Unlike BRCA1, BARD1 germline pathogenic variants are found in <1% of breast cancer patients. As a result, there is insufficient epidemiologic evidence on the role of BARD1 as a breast cancer susceptibility gene. Moreover, the evidence can sometimes be controversial, further impeding our understanding of the clinical relevance of BARD1. For example, one of the most extensively studied BARD1 germline variants, **c.1670G>C**, was shown to be associated with a two- to fourfold increased breast cancer risk in some studies but not in others. Given that BARD1 is a low-penetrance gene, its pathogenic variants likely increased the cancer risk in concert with other low- to moderate-penetrance gene alleles. The interaction among the various genetic risk loci might have led to the varying penetrance estimations observed in the various studies. Overall, existing epidemiologic data are scarce and inconsistent. Functional studies can help circumvent this by reclassifying germline variants:hence, increasing the number of pathogenic variants available for inclusion in epidemiologic studies.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study provides a broad survey of germline DNA‑repair gene variants reported in Latin American breast‑cancer cohorts, including BARD1 c.1670G>C, but does not offer interpretation of its clinical significance.
article_mentions.title: Landscape of Germline Mutations in DNA Repair Genes for Breast Cancer in Latin America: Opportunities for PARP-Like Inhibitors and Immunotherapy

mentions.quoted_text: The BARD1 protein heterodimerizes with BRCA1 and induces translocation and retention of BRCA1 into the nucleus. The **c.1670G>C** (rs28997576) variant was assessed in case-control studies from several populations. In Chile, this variant was associated with an increased breast cancer risk in families with strong family history. Two other variants were detected in a cohort of 124 Peruvian triple-negative breast cancer patients: the pathogenic c.334C>T (rs758972589) variant and the probably pathogenic c.1622C>A (rs777937955) variant.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The review notes that the BARD1 c.1670G>C variant is associated with increased breast cancer risk in Chilean families with strong family history, highlighting its potential role in breast cancer susceptibility in Latin America.
article_mentions.title: Genetic Epidemiology of Breast Cancer in Latin America

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study screened non‑MMR cancer susceptibility genes in families with colorectal cancer, reporting a likely pathogenic CHEK2 variant and several VUS, including BARD1 c.1670G>C, without further interpretation of the BARD1 variant's significance.
article_mentions.title: Identification of genetic variants for clinical management of familial colorectal tumors
